app.title: Busicom 141-PF Calculator
app.subtitle: Explore computing from keypress to transistor — powered by the Intel 4004 (1971)
calculator.display.label: 13-digit display
cpu.title: Intel 4004 CPU State
alu.title: 4-Bit ALU — Ripple Carry Adder
alu.description: The Intel 4004's ALU adds two 4-bit numbers using a chain of four full adders. Each full adder processes one bit position, passing its carry output to the next adder — the 'ripple' in ripple carry.
alu.fullAdder.title: Full Adder
alu.fullAdder.description: Each full adder is built from 2 XOR gates, 2 AND gates, and 1 OR gate. It takes two input bits and a carry-in, producing a sum bit and carry-out.
alu.noAluOp: The current instruction does not use the ALU. Execute an ADD, SUB, INC, or DAC instruction to see the adder chain in action.
gate.title: Logic Gate Activations
gate.description: Every computation flows through physical logic gates — AND, OR, NOT, and XOR. This view shows which gates activated for the current instruction.
gate.decoder.description: The decoder is a tree of AND and NOT gates that pattern-matches the opcode bits to identify the instruction type.
gate.and.description: Output is 1 only when ALL inputs are 1. Built from two transistors in series.
gate.or.description: Output is 1 when ANY input is 1. Built from two transistors in parallel.
gate.not.description: Output is the opposite of the input. The simplest gate — one PMOS and one NMOS transistor.
gate.xor.description: Output is 1 when inputs DIFFER. Used for addition (the sum bit of a half adder is XOR).
transistor.title: CMOS Transistor Level
transistor.description: At the very bottom of the computing stack: CMOS transistors. Every logic gate is built from pairs of NMOS (pull-down) and PMOS (pull-up) transistors.
transistor.selectGate: Select a gate from Layer 4 to inspect its transistor implementation.
transistor.nmos.description: Conducts when gate voltage is HIGH. Used in pull-down networks (connects output to GND).
transistor.pmos.description: Conducts when gate voltage is LOW. Used in pull-up networks (connects output to Vdd).
footer.credit: Simulating the Intel 4004 (1971) — the first commercial microprocessor, designed for the Busicom 141-PF calculator
